title: Terraform Cheat Sheet
description: 100+ Terraform commands for init, plan, apply, state, workspaces, import, and more. Includes an Error Message Lookup section. Verified against official HashiCorp docs.

errorLookup.title: Error Message Lookup
errorLookup.subtitle: Paste the error you are seeing and find the command that fixes it. These are the most searched Terraform errors on Stack Overflow with combined views in the millions.

errors.errorText: Error acquiring the state lock: ConditionalCheckFailedException
errors.whatItMeans: Another Terraform process is holding the state lock in DynamoDB. This happens when a previous run crashed or was interrupted without releasing the lock.
errors.fixNote: Get the LOCK_ID from the error message output. Only run this if you are certain no other legitimate Terraform process is running. Check your DynamoDB lock table first.

errors.whatItMeans: Your state file references a provider that is no longer configured in your current Terraform files. Usually happens after reorganizing code or moving resources between modules.
errors.fixNote: If init does not fix it you may need to remove the orphaned provider from state with terraform state rm.

errors.errorText: Error: Required plugins are not installed
errors.whatItMeans: The .terraform.lock.hcl file exists but the actual provider binaries are missing from .terraform/providers. Common after cloning a repo or cleaning the directory.
errors.fixNote: Always run terraform init after cloning a repo or after any change to required_providers blocks.

errors.whatItMeans: Two arguments in the same resource block are mutually exclusive. Common with security group rules that have both inline rules and separate rule resources.
errors.fixNote: Run terraform validate to get the exact line and argument names. Remove one of the conflicting arguments from the resource block.

errors.errorText: No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration.
errors.whatItMeans: This is not an error. Terraform found the real infrastructure matches what is in your .tf files exactly. Nothing needs to be applied.
errors.fixNote: Run terraform show to see the current state of all managed resources. If you expected changes, check that you saved your .tf files before running plan.

errors.errorText: Error: Resource not found in state
errors.whatItMeans: You are targeting a resource with -target or trying to import into a resource address that does not exist in your current state.
errors.fixNote: Run terraform state list to see all resource addresses in state. Copy the exact address including module path if the resource is inside a module.

commands.title: Initialize a working directory

flags.description: Upgrades all providers to the latest version allowed by version constraints
Reinitializes the backend, ignoring any existing configuration
Skips backend initialization

commands.useCase: Run this first in any new Terraform directory, after cloning a repo, or after adding a new provider.
commands.beginnerNote: Always run terraform init after any change to required_providers or backend blocks. The most common cause of confusing errors is forgetting this step.

commands.title: Validate configuration syntax

flags.description: Outputs validation results as JSON for use in CI pipelines

commands.useCase: Check your configuration for syntax errors and basic logic problems before running plan.
commands.beginnerNote: Validate only checks syntax and basic logic. It does not check whether your AWS resources actually exist or whether your IAM permissions allow the actions you are requesting.

commands.title: Format configuration files

flags.description: Formats all .tf files in subdirectories as well
Exits with a non-zero status if files are not formatted, without changing them (use in CI)
Shows the diff of what would change

commands.useCase: Format all .tf files in the current directory to the canonical Terraform style.
commands.beginnerNote: Run terraform fmt before every commit. Many teams enforce this in CI with terraform fmt -check and fail the pipeline if files are not formatted.

commands.title: Preview infrastructure changes

flags.description: Saves the plan to a file so apply can use the exact same plan
Limits the plan to one specific resource
Loads variable values from a file
Shows what terraform destroy would do

commands.useCase: Preview every change Terraform will make before making any of them.
commands.beginnerNote: Always save the plan with -out=tfplan in CI pipelines so the apply step uses the exact plan that was reviewed. Without -out, a new plan is generated at apply time which may differ from what was reviewed.

commands.title: Apply infrastructure changes

flags.description: Skips the confirmation prompt (use in CI only)
Applies a previously saved plan file passed as an argument
Applies only one specific resource

commands.useCase: Apply the changes shown in the last plan to your real infrastructure.
commands.beginnerNote: Never use -auto-approve in manual workflows. Only use it in CI pipelines where the plan has already been reviewed and approved by a human.

commands.title: Destroy all managed infrastructure

flags.description: Skips the confirmation prompt
Destroys only one resource

commands.useCase: Destroy all resources managed by the current Terraform configuration.
commands.beginnerNote: This permanently deletes real infrastructure and there is no undo. Always run terraform plan -destroy first to see exactly what will be deleted.

commands.title: Preview a destroy operation
commands.useCase: Preview exactly what terraform destroy would delete before actually deleting anything.

commands.title: Read output values

flags.description: Outputs all values as JSON for use in scripts
Outputs a single value as a plain string with no quotes (use in shell scripts)
Shows only that specific output value

commands.useCase: Read the output values from a completed apply, such as a load balancer URL or database endpoint.
commands.beginnerNote: Outputs are only available after a successful apply. If you just ran init or plan, there are no outputs yet.

commands.title: Show the current state in human-readable form

flags.description: Outputs state as JSON for use in scripts
Shows what a saved plan file would do

commands.useCase: Display the current state of all managed resources in a human readable format.

commands.title: Sync state with real infrastructure
commands.useCase: Sync the Terraform state file with the real state of your infrastructure without making any changes.
commands.beginnerNote: terraform refresh is deprecated as a standalone command in recent Terraform versions. Use terraform apply -refresh-only instead, which shows you what would change in state before committing.

commands.title: Update state without changing infrastructure
commands.useCase: Update the state file to match the real infrastructure without making any infrastructure changes.

commands.title: Generate a resource dependency graph
commands.useCase: Generate a visual dependency graph of all resources showing which resources depend on which others.
commands.beginnerNote: Requires graphviz to be installed locally. The output is dot format and must be piped to a renderer.

commands.title: Check the installed Terraform version
commands.useCase: Check which version of Terraform is installed and which providers are being used.

commands.title: Get plan output as machine-readable JSON
commands.useCase: Stream the plan as structured JSON for tooling that renders or analyzes plan output, such as CI dashboards or policy checks.
commands.beginnerNote: The JSON stream is a series of newline-delimited JSON objects, not a single JSON document, so it needs a streaming parser rather than a plain JSON.parse.

commands.title: Get apply output as machine-readable JSON
commands.useCase: Stream apply progress as structured JSON, useful for building a custom deployment UI or parsing results in CI.

commands.title: Check version information as JSON
commands.useCase: Read the Terraform and provider versions programmatically in a script instead of parsing plain text output.

commands.title: Apply without interactive prompts
commands.useCase: Run apply in a non-interactive environment such as CI, where there is no terminal available to answer a variable prompt.
commands.beginnerNote: If a required variable has no default and no value is supplied, -input=false makes apply fail immediately with a clear error instead of hanging while it waits for input that will never come.

commands.title: Plan without interactive prompts
commands.useCase: Run plan in CI where no terminal is available to answer a missing variable prompt.

commands.title: Read outputs without ANSI color codes
commands.useCase: Print output values without terminal color codes so the result is clean when piped into a log file or another tool.

commands.title: List all resources in state

flags.description: Uses a specific state file instead of the default

commands.useCase: List every resource currently tracked in the Terraform state file.
commands.beginnerNote: If you get an error about a resource not existing, run this first to see the exact address including module path.

commands.title: Show a resource's full attributes
commands.useCase: Show the full attributes of one specific resource as stored in state.
commands.beginnerNote: The resource address must match exactly, including module path. Use terraform state list first to find the correct address.

commands.title: Rename or move a resource in state
commands.useCase: Rename a resource in state without destroying and recreating it, or move a resource into or out of a module.
commands.beginnerNote: Always run terraform plan after a state mv to confirm Terraform sees the resource correctly in its new location before running apply.

commands.title: Stop tracking a resource in state
commands.useCase: Remove a resource from state without destroying the real infrastructure, useful when you want Terraform to stop managing a resource.
commands.beginnerNote: The real infrastructure is not deleted when you remove it from state. Terraform simply stops tracking it, but the next terraform plan will try to create it again unless you also remove it from your .tf files.

commands.title: Download the current remote state
commands.useCase: Download and print the current remote state as JSON, useful for inspecting or backing up state.

commands.title: Upload a local state file to the backend
commands.useCase: Upload a local state file to the remote backend, used for disaster recovery.
commands.beginnerNote: Pushing an incorrect state file can corrupt your infrastructure tracking. Always back up the current remote state with terraform state pull before pushing.

commands.title: Release a stuck state lock
commands.useCase: Release a stuck state lock when a previous Terraform run crashed and left the lock in DynamoDB.
commands.beginnerNote: Only run this if you are absolutely certain no other Terraform process is legitimately holding the lock. Check your DynamoDB lock table first and confirm no other pipeline is running.

commands.title: Update state to a new provider source
commands.useCase: Update state to reference a renamed or moved provider without destroying resources.

commands.title: Mark a resource for forced recreation
commands.useCase: Mark a resource as tainted so the next apply destroys and recreates it, useful when a resource is broken in a way Terraform cannot detect.
commands.beginnerNote: Tainting a resource does not change anything immediately. It only affects the next plan or apply, and in modern Terraform the -replace flag on apply is the recommended alternative since it does not require a separate command run first.

commands.title: Remove a taint mark from a resource
commands.useCase: Undo a taint mark on a resource so the next apply leaves it alone instead of recreating it.

commands.title: Force a specific resource to be recreated
commands.useCase: Force Terraform to destroy and recreate a single resource in one step, the modern replacement for the separate taint command.
commands.beginnerNote: This still runs a full plan and apply, so review the plan output before confirming since -replace can be combined with other pending changes in the same apply.

commands.title: Plan without refreshing state first
commands.useCase: Skip the state refresh step before planning, which speeds up plans significantly in large configurations with many resources.
commands.beginnerNote: The plan may miss drift that happened outside Terraform since the last refresh, so only use this for speed in situations where you already trust the current state.

commands.title: Apply without acquiring a state lock
commands.useCase: Disable state locking during apply, used when the configured backend does not support locking or to work around a stuck lock in an emergency.
commands.beginnerNote: Disabling locking removes the safety net that prevents two people from applying at the same time, which can corrupt state if anyone else is running Terraform concurrently.

commands.title: Wait longer for a state lock
commands.useCase: Wait up to the given duration for the state lock to become available instead of failing immediately, useful when a previous run is still finishing.

commands.title: Refresh state for a single resource
commands.useCase: Update state for one specific resource to match reality without touching the rest of the infrastructure or state.

commands.title: Find a resource by its real cloud ID
commands.useCase: Find which Terraform resource address corresponds to a real cloud resource ID found in a console alert or another team's report.

commands.title: Remove an entire module from state
commands.useCase: Remove every resource inside a module from state at once, such as when deleting a module block but wanting to keep the underlying infrastructure untouched.
commands.beginnerNote: This stops Terraform from tracking every resource the module created, so make sure the plan afterward does not try to recreate them because the module block is still present in your configuration.

commands.title: Destroy one specific resource
commands.useCase: Destroy a single resource without touching the rest of the infrastructure managed by the same configuration.
commands.beginnerNote: -target is meant for exceptional situations, not routine use. Repeatedly targeting individual resources instead of applying the full configuration can let real infrastructure drift away from what your .tf files describe.

commands.title: Move a resource into a separate state file
commands.useCase: Move a resource out of the current state into a different state file entirely, such as when splitting one large configuration into several smaller ones.

commands.title: Back up remote state to a local file
commands.useCase: Create a manual local backup of the current remote state before running a risky operation like state rm, state mv, or force-unlock.
commands.beginnerNote: Get in the habit of running this before any command that rewrites state directly, since it gives you something to restore from with terraform state push if something goes wrong.

commands.title: Plan without acquiring a state lock
commands.useCase: Skip acquiring the state lock during plan, useful when a backend does not support locking or when read-only inspection is needed while a lock is held elsewhere.

commands.title: Inspect a resource inside a module
commands.useCase: Show the attributes of a resource that lives inside a module rather than at the root of the configuration.
commands.beginnerNote: The quotes around the module address are required in most shells because the dots and brackets in a nested module path can otherwise be misinterpreted by the shell.

commands.title: Apply several specific resources at once
commands.useCase: Apply changes to more than one specific resource by repeating the -target flag, without applying the rest of the configuration.

commands.title: Inspect a local backup state file
commands.useCase: List resources from a specific local state file, such as the automatic .tfstate.backup Terraform writes before overwriting state, instead of the active remote state.

commands.title: Force push a state file, bypassing safety checks
commands.useCase: Override the remote state's serial and lineage checks when pushing, used only when recovering from a corrupted or intentionally divergent local state.
commands.beginnerNote: This bypasses the exact safety check that normally prevents you from accidentally overwriting newer state with an older copy, so confirm you actually have the most current state before using -force.

commands.title: Refresh state for one resource (legacy syntax)
commands.useCase: Sync state for a single resource using the older refresh command, seen in scripts written before apply -refresh-only existed.
commands.beginnerNote: Prefer terraform apply -refresh-only -target=resource_type.name in current Terraform versions, since standalone refresh is deprecated and offers no preview of what will change before committing it.

commands.title: List all workspaces
commands.useCase: List all workspaces. The current one is marked with an asterisk.

commands.title: Create a new workspace
commands.useCase: Create a new workspace to manage a separate state for the same configuration, for example dev, staging, and production.
commands.beginnerNote: Creating a new workspace does not copy any existing resources. Each workspace has a completely separate state file.

commands.title: Switch to a different workspace
commands.useCase: Switch to a different workspace before running plan or apply.
commands.beginnerNote: Always check which workspace is active with terraform workspace show before running any destructive commands. Running terraform destroy in production when you thought you were in dev is catastrophic.

commands.title: Show the current workspace
commands.useCase: Print the name of the currently active workspace.
commands.beginnerNote: Run this before any destructive operation to confirm you are in the right workspace.

commands.title: Delete a workspace
commands.useCase: Delete a workspace after you have destroyed all its resources.
commands.beginnerNote: You must destroy all resources in a workspace before deleting it, and you cannot delete the default workspace.

commands.title: Create a workspace seeded from an existing state file
commands.useCase: Create a new workspace pre-populated from an existing state file instead of starting empty, useful when converting a single-workspace setup into multiple workspaces.

commands.title: Select a workspace, creating it if needed
commands.useCase: Switch to a workspace in a single command, automatically creating it first if it does not already exist, which avoids a separate new step in CI pipelines.

commands.title: Import an existing resource into state
commands.useCase: Import an existing real infrastructure resource into Terraform state so Terraform can start managing it.
commands.beginnerNote: Importing a resource only updates state. It does not write the corresponding .tf configuration for you. Write the resource block in your .tf files manually before importing, and make sure the configuration matches the real resource or the next plan will show changes.

commands.title: Generate configuration for imported resources
commands.useCase: Automatically generate the .tf configuration for resources being imported, available in Terraform 1.5 and later.
commands.beginnerNote: Generated configuration is a starting point, not a finished product. Always review and clean up the generated .tf file before committing it.

commands.title: Generate the provider dependency lock file

flags.description: Locks for a specific platform

commands.useCase: Generate or update the .terraform.lock.hcl file to pin provider versions for reproducible builds.
commands.beginnerNote: The .terraform.lock.hcl file should be committed to git. This was one of the most upvoted Terraform Stack Overflow questions.

commands.title: Download providers for offline use
commands.useCase: Download all required providers to a local directory for use in air-gapped or offline environments.

commands.title: Import a resource that needs variable values
commands.useCase: Import a resource whose configuration references a variable, so Terraform can evaluate the resource address correctly during the import.

commands.title: Import a resource into a module address
commands.useCase: Import an existing resource directly into a nested module address instead of the root module.
commands.beginnerNote: The quotes around the module address are required in most shells, since the dots in a nested module path can otherwise be misinterpreted.

commands.title: Mirror providers for a specific platform
commands.useCase: Download only the provider binaries needed for a specific operating system and architecture, useful when preparing a mirror for a Linux CI runner from a different machine.

commands.title: Generate configuration for one imported resource
commands.useCase: Generate configuration for a single resource being imported instead of every resource pending import at once.

commands.title: Open an interactive expression console
commands.useCase: Open an interactive console to evaluate Terraform expressions, test functions, and inspect values before using them in configuration.
commands.beginnerNote: Type exit or press Ctrl-D to quit. This is the fastest way to test a complex expression before putting it in your code.

commands.title: Get all outputs as JSON
commands.useCase: Get all output values as JSON for use in scripts or to pass values to another tool.

commands.title: Pass a single variable value on the command line
commands.useCase: Pass a single variable value on the command line for a quick test without editing a .tfvars file.
commands.beginnerNote: For multiple variables, use a .tfvars file with -var-file instead of multiple -var flags.

commands.title: Apply with variables from a specific file
commands.useCase: Apply using variable values from a specific .tfvars file, such as production.tfvars.
commands.beginnerNote: Never commit .tfvars files containing secrets to git. Use environment variables or a secrets manager for sensitive values.

commands.title: Inspect specific resource attributes with jq
commands.useCase: Pipe the structured state output into jq to extract exactly the resource attributes you need, without scrolling through the full human-readable show output.

commands.title: Read a single output as a raw shell value
commands.useCase: Read a single named output value with no surrounding quotes, ready to pipe directly into another command or store in a shell variable.
commands.beginnerNote: Terraform still prints sensitive outputs in plain text with -raw. Be careful about where the output ends up, such as shell history or CI logs.

commands.title: Preview a plan for a specific environment
commands.useCase: Preview the changes for a specific environment's variable values before applying, such as checking staging.tfvars separately from production.tfvars.

commands.title: Evaluate a single expression non-interactively
commands.useCase: Pipe a single expression into terraform console from a script to evaluate one value without opening an interactive session.

commands.title: Pass multiple variables on the command line
commands.useCase: Pass several individual variable values by repeating the -var flag, useful for a quick test with more than one override.

commands.title: Download referenced modules

flags.description: Downloads the latest version of modules within version constraints

commands.useCase: Download and install all modules referenced in your configuration without reinitializing the backend.
commands.beginnerNote: terraform init also runs terraform get. Use terraform get -update specifically when you want to update modules without touching the backend configuration.

commands.title: Show provider requirements
commands.useCase: Show the provider requirements for the current configuration and which module requires each provider.

commands.title: Upgrade providers and modules
commands.useCase: Upgrade all providers and modules to the latest versions allowed by your version constraints.
commands.beginnerNote: This updates the .terraform.lock.hcl file. Commit the updated lock file to git so teammates use the same provider versions.

commands.title: Initialize a directory with OpenTofu
commands.useCase: OpenTofu is an open source fork of Terraform with an Apache 2.0 license. Its CLI commands are identical to Terraform but use the tofu binary instead of terraform.
commands.beginnerNote: If your team is migrating from Terraform to OpenTofu for license reasons, every command in this cheat sheet works identically with tofu replacing terraform in the command.

commands.title: Dump the full provider schema
commands.useCase: Dump every resource and data source argument for every provider in the configuration as JSON, useful for tooling, documentation generation, or checking exactly what a resource type accepts.

commands.title: Install providers from a local directory
commands.useCase: Initialize using providers from a local directory instead of the registry, used in air-gapped environments alongside terraform providers mirror.
commands.beginnerNote: When -plugin-dir is set, Terraform stops checking the registry entirely and dependency lock file verification is skipped, so make sure the directory actually has every provider version you need.

commands.title: Lock providers for every platform your team uses
commands.useCase: Lock provider checksums for multiple operating systems and architectures at once, since developers on macOS and CI running on Linux both need matching entries in the lock file.
commands.beginnerNote: Running terraform providers lock with no -platform flag only adds entries for the platform you ran it on, which is why teams with mixed development machines and CI runners need to repeat the flag for each platform.

commands.title: Update modules to their latest allowed version
commands.useCase: Re-download every referenced module at the newest version allowed by its version constraint, without touching provider or backend configuration.

commands.title: Pass backend configuration on the command line
commands.useCase: Provide backend configuration values on the command line instead of hardcoding them in the backend block, useful for keeping secrets out of .tf files.

commands.title: Migrate state to a new backend
commands.useCase: Migrate existing state to a new backend when you are changing backend configuration.
commands.beginnerNote: Always back up your current state file before migrating backends. This copies state to the new location, but if anything goes wrong you need the backup.

commands.title: Reinitialize the backend from scratch
commands.useCase: Reinitialize the backend, ignoring any existing backend configuration, used when switching between backends.

commands.title: Initialize without interactive prompts
commands.useCase: Disable interactive prompts for backend configuration values, required in CI pipelines since there is no terminal to answer them.
commands.beginnerNote: Without -input=false, a CI job with a partially configured backend can appear to hang forever while it silently waits for input that will never arrive.

commands.title: Initialize without downloading modules
commands.useCase: Skip downloading modules during init, useful when only reconfiguring the backend and modules are already present.

commands.title: Initialize without configuring a backend
commands.useCase: Skip backend initialization entirely, useful when only working with modules locally or testing configuration without connecting to remote state.
commands.beginnerNote: Running plan or apply after -backend=false still fails, since those commands need a working backend. Use this only for quick local checks like validate or fmt.

commands.title: Migrate backends without a confirmation prompt
commands.useCase: Skip the interactive yes or no prompt that normally appears when copying state to a new backend, useful for scripted backend migrations in CI.
commands.beginnerNote: This skips a safety confirmation, so only use it in a scripted migration you have already tested manually once.

commands.title: Switch backends and upgrade providers together
commands.useCase: Reconfigure the backend and upgrade providers to the latest allowed versions in a single init call, common when switching backends and bumping provider versions at the same time.

commands.title: Wait longer for the backend lock during init
commands.useCase: Wait up to the given duration for the backend's state lock during init instead of failing immediately, useful when init runs right after another pipeline stage that briefly holds the lock.

commands.title: Enable verbose debug logging
commands.useCase: Enable verbose debug logging to diagnose provider errors, API call failures, or unexpected behavior.
commands.beginnerNote: The output is extremely verbose. Pipe it to a file with TF_LOG=DEBUG terraform plan 2> debug.log and search for the specific error rather than reading the whole output.

commands.title: Write debug logs to a file
commands.command: export TF_LOG=DEBUG && export TF_LOG_PATH=./terraform.log && terraform plan
commands.useCase: Write debug logs to a file instead of printing them to the terminal.

commands.title: Validate and output results as JSON
commands.useCase: Validate configuration and output results as JSON for parsing in CI pipelines.

commands.title: Get a distinct exit code for pending changes
commands.useCase: Exit with code 0 if there are no changes, 1 if there is an error, and 2 if there are changes, useful in CI to distinguish success with no changes from success with pending changes.
commands.beginnerNote: Without -detailed-exitcode, terraform plan exits with 0 whether or not there are changes, so CI cannot tell the difference between a plan that succeeded with nothing to do and one that succeeded with changes waiting to be applied.

commands.title: Limit concurrent operations
commands.useCase: Limit the number of concurrent operations Terraform performs, the default is 10, useful for rate-limited APIs.
commands.beginnerNote: Reducing parallelism slows down applies but can prevent API throttling errors from cloud providers.

commands.title: Enable the most verbose logging level
commands.useCase: Enable trace level logging, one level more detailed than DEBUG, used when debug logs are not detailed enough to diagnose a provider crash.

commands.title: Collapse repeated warnings into a summary
commands.useCase: Collapse repeated warning messages into a single summary line, useful for large configurations where warnings would otherwise flood CI output.

commands.title: Validate without ANSI color codes
commands.useCase: Disable ANSI color codes in validate output, needed when piping output into a log file or a CI system that does not render terminal colors.
commands.beginnerNote: Without -no-color, logs captured by some CI systems show raw escape codes mixed into the text instead of clean output.

commands.title: Scope debug logging to provider communication
commands.useCase: Scope debug logging to only the communication between Terraform and provider plugins, producing a smaller and more focused log when the bug is clearly inside a provider.

commands.title: Scope debug logging to Terraform core
commands.useCase: Scope debug logging to only Terraform core internals, excluding provider chatter, useful when the bug is in Terraform itself rather than a provider.

commands.title: Run a safe CI apply step
commands.useCase: Combine auto approval with disabled input prompts, the standard safe pairing for a CI pipeline apply step since a missing variable fails loudly instead of hanging.
commands.beginnerNote: Only use -auto-approve in a pipeline where the plan has already been reviewed as a separate step. Never combine it with a plan that has not been seen by a human.

commands.title: Check a state command's exit code in a script
commands.useCase: Check whether a state command succeeded from within a script by testing its exit code, without needing to parse the actual output.

commands.title: Combine detailed exit codes with clean CI output
commands.useCase: Combine a parseable exit code with disabled color output, the standard combination for CI systems that need both a reliable exit code and clean log text.

commands.title: Isolate the local Terraform working directory
commands.useCase: Override where Terraform stores its local .terraform working directory, used to isolate concurrent Terraform runs in the same CI workspace so parallel jobs do not overwrite each other's provider cache.

commands.title: Inject default flags into every plan
commands.useCase: Automatically inject a flag into every invocation of a specific subcommand, useful for enforcing -no-color or -input=false across an entire CI system without editing every script.

commands.title: Tell Terraform it is running in CI
commands.useCase: Tell Terraform it is running in a CI system rather than interactively, which trims some of the suggested next-command hints from output since no human is present to act on them.
